Hands-on Exercise 2

Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals

Author

Thi Thanh Van Tran

Published

April 22, 2026

Modified

April 25, 2026

1 Overview

In this hands-on exercise, I will explore several ggplot2 extensions for creating more elegant and effective statistical graphics. By the end of this exercise, I will be able to:

  • Control the placement of annotation on a graph by using functions provided in ggrepel package
  • Create professional publication quality figures by using functions provided in ggthemes and hrbrthemes packages
  • Plot composite figures by combining ggplot2 graphs using patchwork package

Additionally, I will conduct comprehensive data-driven analysis with dynamic insights extracted directly from the examination results.

2 Getting Started

2.1 Installing and Loading Required Libraries

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse, 
               GGally, viridis, ggridges, scales, gridExtra)

2.2 Importing Data

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

2.3 Initial Data Exploration

glimpse(exam_data)
Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…
summary(exam_data)
      ID               CLASS              GENDER              RACE          
 Length:322         Length:322         Length:322         Length:322        
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
    ENGLISH          MATHS          SCIENCE     
 Min.   :21.00   Min.   : 9.00   Min.   :15.00  
 1st Qu.:59.00   1st Qu.:58.00   1st Qu.:49.25  
 Median :70.00   Median :74.00   Median :65.00  
 Mean   :67.18   Mean   :69.33   Mean   :61.16

 Max.   :96.00   Max.   :99.00   Max.   :96.00

2.3.1 Data Quality Assessment

  • Results
  • Code
Total students: 322 
Complete cases: 322 
Completeness rate: 100 %
Missing values per column:
     ID   CLASS  GENDER    RACE ENGLISH   MATHS SCIENCE 
      0       0       0       0       0       0       0 
# Calculate data quality metrics
n_students <- nrow(exam_data)
n_complete_cases <- sum(complete.cases(exam_data))
missing_counts <- colSums(is.na(exam_data))
completeness_rate <- (n_complete_cases / n_students) * 100

# Display results
cat("Total students:", n_students, "\n")
cat("Complete cases:", n_complete_cases, "\n")
cat("Completeness rate:", round(completeness_rate, 1), "%\n")
cat("Missing values per column:\n")
print(missing_counts)
NoteData Quality Summary

The dataset contains 322 students with 7 variables.

Data completeness: 100% of cases have no missing values.

No missing values detected - dataset is complete and ready for analysis.

2.3.2 Categorical Variable Distribution

  • Results
  • Code
Gender Distribution:

Female   Male 
   170    152 

Race Distribution:

Chinese  Indian   Malay  Others 
    193      12     108       9 

Class Distribution:

3A 3B 3C 3D 3E 3F 3G 3H 3I 
40 38 39 38 38 39 34 33 23 
# Analyze categorical distributions
gender_dist <- table(exam_data$GENDER)
race_dist <- table(exam_data$RACE)
class_dist <- table(exam_data$CLASS)

# Calculate proportions
gender_prop <- prop.table(gender_dist) * 100
race_prop <- prop.table(race_dist) * 100
class_prop <- prop.table(class_dist) * 100

# Display
cat("Gender Distribution:\n")
print(gender_dist)
cat("\nRace Distribution:\n")
print(race_dist)
cat("\nClass Distribution:\n")
print(class_dist)
TipSample Composition Analysis

Gender Balance: The dataset contains 170 (52.8%) Female students and 152 (47.2%) Male students. The gender distribution is relatively balanced.

Diversity: The sample includes 4 different racial groups, with Chinese being the largest group at 59.9%.

Class Distribution: Students are distributed across 9 classes, with class sizes ranging from 23 to 40 students.

3 Beyond ggplot2 Annotation: ggrepel

3.1 The Problem: Overlapping Labels

  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), hjust = .5, vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
  • Results
  • Code
Correlation between Maths and English: 0.831
# Calculate correlation for interpretation
cor_maths_english <- cor(exam_data$MATHS, exam_data$ENGLISH)
cat("Correlation between Maths and English:", round(cor_maths_english, 3))
WarningVisualization Issue Identified

The scatter plot above demonstrates a critical labeling problem: with 322 data points, the labels overlap significantly, making individual student IDs impossible to read.

The plot does show a positive correlation (r = 0.831) between Mathematics and English scores, but the overlapping labels obscure this insight and make the visualization ineffective for identifying individual students.

3.2 The Solution: Using ggrepel

  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
TipImprovement Achieved

Using geom_label_repel() instead of geom_label() successfully eliminates label overlap through intelligent automatic positioning. Each of the 322 student IDs is now readable and traceable to its corresponding data point, while maintaining the visualization of the strong positive correlation (r = 0.831) between subjects.

3.3 Extended Analysis: Selective Labeling Strategy

  • Results
  • Code
Top 10% threshold: 254 
Bottom 10% threshold: 118.2 
Number of labeled students: 68 
# Calculate performance metrics
exam_data_labeled <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    total_score = MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE,
    performance_label = case_when(
      total_score >= quantile(total_score, 0.9) ~ ID,
      total_score <= quantile(total_score, 0.1) ~ ID,
      TRUE ~ ""
    )
  )

# Identify thresholds
top_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.9)
bottom_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.1)
n_labeled <- sum(exam_data_labeled$performance_label != "")

cat("Top 10% threshold:", round(top_10_threshold, 1), "\n")
cat("Bottom 10% threshold:", round(bottom_10_threshold, 1), "\n")
cat("Number of labeled students:", n_labeled, "\n")
  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data_labeled, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = total_score), size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5, color = "darkblue") +  
  geom_label_repel(
    aes(label = performance_label), 
    fontface = "bold",
    box.padding = 0.5,
    point.padding = 0.3,
    segment.color = 'grey50',
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths: Top/Bottom 10% Performers Labeled",
    subtitle = "Color gradient shows total score across all subjects",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Total Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
NoteStrategic Labeling Analysis

Selective Approach: Instead of labeling all 322 students, this visualization strategically labels only 68 students (21.1% of the sample) - specifically the top and bottom 10% performers.

Performance Thresholds: - Top 10%: Total score ≥ 254 points - Bottom 10%: Total score ≤ 118.2 points

Key Insight: The color gradient reveals that high performers in Mathematics tend to also score high in English (note the concentration of yellow/bright points in the upper-right quadrant), confirming the strong correlation (r = 0.831) and suggesting that academic ability is relatively consistent across subjects for exceptional students.

3.4 Gender-Based Performance Comparison

  • Results
  • Code
# A tibble: 2 × 5
  GENDER     n cor_me mean_maths mean_english
  <chr>  <int>  <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl>
1 Female   170  0.853       70.0         69.8
2 Male     152  0.828       68.6         64.3
# Calculate gender-specific correlations and statistics
gender_stats <- exam_data %>%
  group_by(GENDER) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    cor_me = cor(MATHS, ENGLISH),
    mean_maths = mean(MATHS),
    mean_english = mean(ENGLISH)
  )

print(gender_stats)
  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH, color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2) +  
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9")) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths Performance by Gender",
    subtitle = "Separate regression lines for each gender group",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Gender"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
  • Results
  • Code
Male slope: 0.595 
Female slope: 0.621 
Slope difference: -0.026 
# Calculate slope differences
lm_male <- lm(ENGLISH ~ MATHS, data = filter(exam_data, GENDER == "Male"))
lm_female <- lm(ENGLISH ~ MATHS, data = filter(exam_data, GENDER == "Female"))
slope_male <- coef(lm_male)[2]
slope_female <- coef(lm_female)[2]

cat("Male slope:", round(slope_male, 3), "\n")
cat("Female slope:", round(slope_female, 3), "\n")
cat("Slope difference:", round(slope_male - slope_female, 3), "\n")
ImportantGender Comparison Insights

Sample Sizes: 152 Male students vs 170 Female students.

Correlation Strength: - Male students: r = 0.828 - Female students: r = 0.853

Performance Patterns: - Mathematics: Males averaged 68.6 vs Females 70 (difference: 1.4 points) - English: Males averaged 64.3 vs Females 69.8 (difference: 5.5 points)

Regression Analysis: The slope of the relationship differs slightly (0.595 for males vs 0.621 for females), suggesting similar performance patterns.

4 Beyond ggplot2 Themes

4.1 Built-in Theme Comparison

  • Results
  • Code
Mathematics Mean: 69.3 
Mathematics Median: 74 
Mathematics SD: 20 
Skewness coefficient: -0.234 
# Calculate distribution statistics for interpretation
maths_mean <- mean(exam_data$MATHS)
maths_median <- median(exam_data$MATHS)
maths_sd <- sd(exam_data$MATHS)
skewness <- (maths_mean - maths_median) / maths_sd

cat("Mathematics Mean:", round(maths_mean, 1), "\n")
cat("Mathematics Median:", round(maths_median, 1), "\n")
cat("Mathematics SD:", round(maths_sd, 1), "\n")
cat("Skewness coefficient:", round(skewness, 3), "\n")
  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
NoteDistribution Characteristics

Central Tendency: Mean = 69.3, Median = 74

Spread: Standard deviation = 20 points

Shape: The distribution shows slight negative skewness (left tail) with skewness coefficient of -0.234.

4.2 Professional Themes: ggthemes

  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()
TipTheme Selection Rationale

The Economist theme provides a professional, publication-ready aesthetic suitable for business and academic contexts. This theme emphasizes clarity and readability, making it ideal for presenting the Mathematics score distribution (μ = 69.3, σ = 20) to stakeholders.

4.3 Typography-Focused: hrbrthemes

  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()
  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18, base_size = 15, grid = "Y")
TipCustomization Impact

The customized theme_ipsum() with larger fonts (axis titles: 18pt, base: 15pt) and Y-only gridlines improves readability while reducing visual clutter. This is particularly effective for presentations where the audience needs to quickly grasp the distribution pattern showing most students cluster around the mean of 69.3 points.

4.4 Theme Effectiveness Comparison

  • Results
  • Code
English Mean: 67.2 
English SD: 14.7 
create_themed_plot <- function(theme_name, theme_func) {
  ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
    geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                   color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
    ggtitle(theme_name) +
    theme_func
}

# Calculate English score statistics
english_mean <- mean(exam_data$ENGLISH)
english_sd <- sd(exam_data$ENGLISH)

cat("English Mean:", round(english_mean, 1), "\n")
cat("English SD:", round(english_sd, 1), "\n")
  • Plot
  • Code
p_minimal <- create_themed_plot("theme_minimal()", theme_minimal())
p_bw <- create_themed_plot("theme_bw()", theme_bw())
p_classic <- create_themed_plot("theme_classic()", theme_classic())
p_wsj <- create_themed_plot("theme_wsj()", theme_wsj())
p_fivethirtyeight <- create_themed_plot("theme_fivethirtyeight()", 
                                        theme_fivethirtyeight())
p_tufte <- create_themed_plot("theme_tufte()", theme_tufte())

grid.arrange(p_minimal, p_bw, p_classic, 
             p_wsj, p_fivethirtyeight, p_tufte, 
             ncol = 3)
ImportantTheme Selection Guidelines

Each theme serves different communication purposes when displaying the English score distribution (μ = 67.2, σ = 14.7):

  • theme_minimal(): Clean, modern - best for digital reports
  • theme_bw(): High contrast - ideal for printing and photocopying
  • theme_classic(): Traditional academic style - suitable for research papers
  • theme_wsj(): Business professional - corporate presentations
  • theme_fivethirtyeight(): Data journalism - blog posts and articles
  • theme_tufte(): Minimalist - when maximizing data-ink ratio is critical

Selection Criteria: Choose based on audience, medium, and context. For this academic analysis of 322 students, theme_minimal() or theme_classic() are most appropriate.

5 Composite Figures with Patchwork

5.1 Creating Component Visualizations

  • Plot
  • Code
p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
p1
  • Plot
  • Code
p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")
p2
  • Plot
  • Code
p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
p3

5.2 Two-Panel Layout

  • Results
  • Code
Subject Mean SD Range
Mathematics 69.33 19.98 90
English 67.18 14.69 75 
# Compare distributions
comparison_stats <- tibble(
  Subject = c("Mathematics", "English"),
  Mean = c(maths_mean, english_mean),
  SD = c(maths_sd, english_sd),
  Range = c(max(exam_data$MATHS) - min(exam_data$MATHS),
            max(exam_data$ENGLISH) - min(exam_data$ENGLISH))
)

knitr::kable(comparison_stats, digits = 2)
  • Plot
  • Code
p1 + p2
NoteSide-by-Side Distribution Comparison

Mathematics: μ = 69.3, σ = 20, range = 90

English: μ = 67.2, σ = 14.7, range = 75

Comparative Insight: Mathematics shows higher average performance by 2.1 points, with greater variability (SD difference: 5.3 points). This suggests more heterogeneous performance in Mathematics.

5.3 Complex Three-Panel Layout

  • Plot
  • Code
(p1 / p2) | p3
TipComprehensive Visual Narrative

This layout effectively tells a complete story:

Left panels (distributions): Show individual subject performance characteristics - Mathematics: 322 students, μ = 69.3 - English: 322 students, μ = 67.2

Right panel (relationship): Reveals strong positive correlation (r = 0.831)

Data-Driven Conclusion: The 0.831 correlation coefficient indicates that approximately 69.1% of variance in English scores can be explained by Mathematics performance, suggesting shared underlying academic abilities.

5.4 Tagged Figure for Academic Reference

  • Plot
  • Code
((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')
NoteFigure Referencing System

Auto-tagging (I, II, III) enables precise referencing in academic writing. For example: “Panel I shows Mathematics distribution with mean 69.3; Panel II displays English distribution; Panel III demonstrates the r = 0.831 correlation between subjects.”

5.5 Inset Visualization

  • Plot
  • Code
p3 + inset_element(p2, left = 0.02, bottom = 0.7, right = 0.5, top = 1)
TipContextual Inset Rationale

The inset histogram provides immediate context for the scatter plot by showing the English score distribution (322 students, μ = 67.2) directly within the correlation visualization. This space-efficient design allows readers to simultaneously assess both the relationship (r = 0.831) and the marginal distribution.

5.6 Themed Composite

  • Plot
  • Code
patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()
TipUnified Theme Application

Applying theme_economist() across all panels creates visual consistency essential for professional presentations. The unified styling helps readers focus on the data patterns (means: Maths 69.3, English 67.2; correlation r = 0.831) rather than being distracted by stylistic inconsistencies.

6 Extended Analysis: Comprehensive Performance Insights

6.1 Multi-Subject Statistical Overview

  • Results
  • Code
Comprehensive Summary Statistics by Subject
Subject Mean Median SD Min Max Range CV
Mathematics 69.33 74 19.98 9 99 90 28.81
English 67.18 70 14.69 21 96 75 21.87
Science 61.16 65 18.18 15 96 81 29.72 
# Calculate comprehensive statistics
science_mean <- mean(exam_data$SCIENCE)
science_sd <- sd(exam_data$SCIENCE)
science_median <- median(exam_data$SCIENCE)
english_median <- median(exam_data$ENGLISH)

summary_stats <- tibble(
  Subject = c("Mathematics", "English", "Science"),
  Mean = c(maths_mean, english_mean, science_mean),
  Median = c(maths_median, english_median, science_median),
  SD = c(maths_sd, english_sd, science_sd),
  Min = c(min(exam_data$MATHS), min(exam_data$ENGLISH), min(exam_data$SCIENCE)),
  Max = c(max(exam_data$MATHS), max(exam_data$ENGLISH), max(exam_data$SCIENCE)),
  Range = Max - Min,
  CV = (SD / Mean) * 100
)

knitr::kable(summary_stats, digits = 2,
             caption = "Comprehensive Summary Statistics by Subject")
  • Results
  • Code
Best performing subject: Mathematics 
Worst performing subject: Science 
Most variable subject: Science 
Least variable subject: English 
# Identify best and worst performing subjects
best_subject <- summary_stats$Subject[which.max(summary_stats$Mean)]
worst_subject <- summary_stats$Subject[which.min(summary_stats$Mean)]
most_variable <- summary_stats$Subject[which.max(summary_stats$CV)]
least_variable <- summary_stats$Subject[which.min(summary_stats$CV)]

cat("Best performing subject:", best_subject, "\n")
cat("Worst performing subject:", worst_subject, "\n")
cat("Most variable subject:", most_variable, "\n")
cat("Least variable subject:", least_variable, "\n")
ImportantComparative Subject Performance Analysis

Highest Performance: Mathematics (μ = 69.3)

Lowest Performance: Science (μ = 61.2)

Performance Gap: 8.2 points between highest and lowest performing subjects

Variability Analysis: - Most Variable: Science (CV = 29.7%) - indicates greatest performance heterogeneity - Least Variable: English (CV = 21.9%) - indicates most consistent performance across students

Key Insight: The coefficient of variation reveals that Science shows 1.4× more relative variability than English, suggesting scientific understanding is more heterogeneous.

6.2 Correlation Matrix Analysis

  • Results
  • Code
Pearson Correlation Matrix: Subject Relationships
MATHS ENGLISH SCIENCE
MATHS 1.000 0.831 0.869
ENGLISH 0.831 1.000 0.878
SCIENCE 0.869 0.878 1.000 
cor_matrix <- exam_data %>%
  select(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE) %>%
  cor()

knitr::kable(round(cor_matrix, 3), 
             caption = "Pearson Correlation Matrix: Subject Relationships")
  • Results
  • Code
Math-English correlation: 0.831 
Math-Science correlation: 0.869 
English-Science correlation: 0.878 
Strongest pair: ES = 0.878 
# Extract correlations
cor_me <- cor_matrix["MATHS", "ENGLISH"]
cor_ms <- cor_matrix["MATHS", "SCIENCE"]
cor_es <- cor_matrix["ENGLISH", "SCIENCE"]

# Determine strongest correlation
cors <- c(ME = cor_me, MS = cor_ms, ES = cor_es)
strongest_pair <- names(cors)[which.max(cors)]
strongest_value <- max(cors)

cat("Math-English correlation:", round(cor_me, 3), "\n")
cat("Math-Science correlation:", round(cor_ms, 3), "\n")
cat("English-Science correlation:", round(cor_es, 3), "\n")
cat("Strongest pair:", strongest_pair, "=", round(strongest_value, 3), "\n")
ImportantInter-Subject Correlation Insights

Strongest Relationship: ES correlation = 0.878

This means 77.1% of variance is shared between these subjects.

All Correlations: - Math-English: r = 0.831 (strong positive) - Math-Science: r = 0.869 (strong positive) - English-Science: r = 0.878 (strong positive)

Educational Implication: All correlations exceed 0.7, indicating strong positive relationships across subjects. Students who excel in one subject typically excel in others, suggesting: 1. Shared cognitive abilities underpin performance 2. Study habits and motivation are consistent across subjects 3. Interventions should target general academic skills rather than subject-specific remediation

6.3 Gender-Based Performance Analysis

  • Results
  • Code
Performance Metrics by Gender
GENDER N Maths_Mean English_Mean Science_Mean Maths_SD English_SD Science_SD Total_Mean
Female 170 69.99 69.76 62.68 18.68 13.60 16.92 202.43
Male 152 68.59 64.30 59.47 21.37 15.36 19.41 192.36 
gender_analysis <- exam_data %>%
  group_by(GENDER) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Maths_Mean = mean(MATHS),
    English_Mean = mean(ENGLISH),
    Science_Mean = mean(SCIENCE),
    Maths_SD = sd(MATHS),
    English_SD = sd(ENGLISH),
    Science_SD = sd(SCIENCE),
    Total_Mean = mean(MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE)
  )

knitr::kable(gender_analysis, digits = 2,
             caption = "Performance Metrics by Gender")
  • Results
  • Code
Maths difference (M-F): -1.4 
English difference (M-F): -5.5 
Science difference (M-F): -3.2 
Total difference (M-F): -10.1 
Better overall gender: Female 
# Calculate differences
male_stats <- gender_analysis %>% filter(GENDER == "Male")
female_stats <- gender_analysis %>% filter(GENDER == "Female")

maths_diff <- male_stats$Maths_Mean - female_stats$Maths_Mean
english_diff <- male_stats$English_Mean - female_stats$English_Mean
science_diff <- male_stats$Science_Mean - female_stats$Science_Mean
total_diff <- male_stats$Total_Mean - female_stats$Total_Mean

# Determine which gender performs better overall
better_gender <- ifelse(total_diff > 0, "Male", "Female")

cat("Maths difference (M-F):", round(maths_diff, 1), "\n")
cat("English difference (M-F):", round(english_diff, 1), "\n")
cat("Science difference (M-F):", round(science_diff, 1), "\n")
cat("Total difference (M-F):", round(total_diff, 1), "\n")
cat("Better overall gender:", better_gender, "\n")
NoteGender Performance Comparison

Sample Distribution: 152 males vs 170 females

Subject-Specific Differences: - Mathematics: Females lead by 1.4 points - English: Females lead by 5.5 points
- Science: Females lead by 3.2 points

Overall Performance: Female students show higher total performance by 10.1 points on average.

Statistical Significance Assessment: With differences of 1.4, 5.5, and 3.2 points across subjects, these represent substantial performance gaps that warrant further investigation.

6.3.1 Gender Performance Visualization

  • Plot
  • Code
exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject",
               values_to = "Score") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Subject, y = Score, fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9")) +
  labs(
    title = "Score Distribution by Subject and Gender",
    subtitle = paste("Comparing", n_students, "students across three subjects"),
    y = "Score",
    x = "Subject"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
  • Results
  • Code
IQR Analysis by Subject and Gender
Subject GENDER Q1 Median Q3 IQR
ENGLISH Female 62 73.0 80 18
ENGLISH Male 54 67.0 75 21
MATHS Female 60 74.0 85 25
MATHS Male 58 75.0 85 27
SCIENCE Female 52 65.0 75 23
SCIENCE Male 45 63.5 74 29 
# Calculate IQR for each group
iqr_analysis <- exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject", values_to = "Score") %>%
  group_by(Subject, GENDER) %>%
  summarise(
    Q1 = quantile(Score, 0.25),
    Median = median(Score),
    Q3 = quantile(Score, 0.75),
    IQR = IQR(Score),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

knitr::kable(iqr_analysis, digits = 2,
             caption = "IQR Analysis by Subject and Gender")
TipBoxplot Distribution Insights

Spread Analysis: The interquartile ranges (IQRs) reveal score concentration patterns:

- **MATHS:** Male IQR = 27, Female IQR = 25 → Males show more variability
- **ENGLISH:** Male IQR = 21, Female IQR = 18 → Males show more variability
- **SCIENCE:** Male IQR = 29, Female IQR = 23 → Males show more variability

Median Comparison: The median lines within boxes show central tendency for each group, revealing females typically outperform males across subjects.

6.4 Race-Based Performance Analysis

  • Results
  • Code
Performance Metrics by Race (Ranked by Total Score)
RACE N Maths_Mean English_Mean Science_Mean Total_Mean Overall_SD
Chinese 193 76.51 70.76 66.99 214.25 41.76
Others 9 69.67 70.11 66.89 206.67 36.83
Indian 12 60.67 60.75 53.25 174.67 59.11
Malay 108 57.44 61.27 51.15 169.86 51.65 
race_analysis <- exam_data %>%
  group_by(RACE) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Maths_Mean = mean(MATHS),
    English_Mean = mean(ENGLISH),
    Science_Mean = mean(SCIENCE),
    Total_Mean = mean(MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE),
    Overall_SD = sd(MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Mean))

knitr::kable(race_analysis, digits = 2,
             caption = "Performance Metrics by Race (Ranked by Total Score)")
  • Results
  • Code
Best performing race: Chinese 
Worst performing race: Malay 
Performance gap: 44.4 points
# Calculate performance gaps
best_race <- race_analysis$RACE[1]
worst_race <- race_analysis$RACE[nrow(race_analysis)]
performance_gap <- race_analysis$Total_Mean[1] - race_analysis$Total_Mean[nrow(race_analysis)]

cat("Best performing race:", best_race, "\n")
cat("Worst performing race:", worst_race, "\n")
cat("Performance gap:", round(performance_gap, 1), "points\n")
ImportantRacial Group Performance Analysis

Highest Performing Group: Chinese (Total Mean = 214.3)

Lowest Performing Group: Malay (Total Mean = 169.9)

Performance Gap: 44.4 points separate the highest and lowest performing racial groups

Distribution of Excellence:

- Chinese: 193 students (59.9%) - Average: 214.3 points
- Others: 9 students (2.8%) - Average: 206.7 points
- Indian: 12 students (3.7%) - Average: 174.7 points
- Malay: 108 students (33.5%) - Average: 169.9 points

Equity Consideration: The 44.4-point gap represents 23.2% of mean total performance, indicating significant performance disparities that merit attention from educators.

6.4.1 Race Performance Visualization

  • Plot
  • Code
exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject",
               values_to = "Score") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, y = Score, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    title = "Score Distribution by Race and Subject",
    subtitle = paste("Performance comparison across", length(unique(exam_data$RACE)), 
                     "racial groups and 3 subjects"),
    y = "Score",
    x = "Race"

Subject Variation Across Racial Groups
Subject Range
MATHS 19.06
SCIENCE 15.84
ENGLISH 10.01 
# Analyze which subject shows most variation across races
subject_variation <- exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject", values_to = "Score") %>%
  group_by(Subject, RACE) %>%
  summarise(Mean = mean(Score), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(Subject) %>%
  summarise(
    Range = max(Mean) - min(Mean),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Range))

             caption = "Subject Variation Across Racial Groups")

Most Variable Subject Across Races: MATHS (range = 19.1 points)

Most Consistent Subject Across Races: ENGLISH (range = 10 points)

The 1.9× greater variability in MATHS suggests that racial background may have differential impact on subject performance, with MATHS showing greater sensitivity to demographic factors.

Performance Metrics by Class (Ranked by Total Score)
CLASS N Maths_Mean English_Mean Science_Mean Total_Mean Total_SD
3A 40 89.92 83.35 81.08 254.35 17.11

class_analysis <- exam_data %>%
  group_by(CLASS) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Maths_Mean = mean(MATHS),
    English_Mean = mean(ENGLISH),
    Science_Mean = mean(SCIENCE),
    Total_Mean = mean(MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE),
    Total_SD = sd(MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE)
  ) %>%

knitr::kable(class_analysis, digits = 2,
             caption = "Performance Metrics by Class (Ranked by Total Score)")
  • Results
  • Code
Best class: 3A 
Worst class: 3I 
Class gap: 157.9 points
CV across classes: 27 %
# Analyze class performance distribution
best_class <- class_analysis$CLASS[1]
worst_class <- class_analysis$CLASS[nrow(class_analysis)]
class_gap <- class_analysis$Total_Mean[1] - class_analysis$Total_Mean[nrow(class_analysis)]

cat("Best class:", best_class, "\n")
cat("Worst class:", worst_class, "\n")
cat("Class gap:", round(class_gap, 1), "points\n")
cat("CV across classes:", round(sd(class_analysis$Total_Mean) / mean(class_analysis$Total_Mean) * 100, 1), "%\n")
WarningClass-Level Performance Disparities

Top Performing Class: 3A (Average: 254.3 points, N = 40)

Lowest Performing Class: 3I (Average: 96.5 points, N = 23)

Inter-Class Gap: 157.9 points

Consistency Analysis: The coefficient of variation across classes is 27%, suggesting significant teaching quality disparities across the 9 classes.

Recommendation: The 157.9-point gap warrants immediate investigation into teaching practices and resource allocation.

6.6 Comprehensive Density Analysis

  • Results
  • Code
Density Statistics by Subject
Subject Peak Spread
ENGLISH 70 19.0
MATHS 74 27.0
SCIENCE 65 25.5 
# Analyze distribution overlap
density_stats <- exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject", values_to = "Score") %>%
  group_by(Subject) %>%
  summarise(
    Peak = median(Score),
    Spread = IQR(Score),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

knitr::kable(density_stats, digits = 2,
             caption = "Density Statistics by Subject")
  • Plot
  • Code
exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject",
               values_to = "Score") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Score, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    title = "Score Distributions Across All Subjects",
    subtitle = paste("Density curves for", n_students, "students in three subjects"),
    x = "Score",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
NoteDistribution Pattern Analysis

Peak Locations (Median Scores):

- ENGLISH: 70 points
- MATHS: 74 points
- SCIENCE: 65 points

Distribution Spread (IQR):

- ENGLISH: 19 points (middle 50% range)
- MATHS: 27 points (middle 50% range)
- SCIENCE: 25.5 points (middle 50% range)

Overlap Interpretation: The substantial overlap in density curves confirms the strong inter-subject correlations observed earlier (0.86 average correlation). This suggests that students’ performance is relatively consistent across subjects rather than showing subject-specific strengths or weaknesses.

6.7 Ridge Plot: Class Performance Distribution

  • Results
  • Code
Distribution Shape by Class
CLASS Mean Median Skew Shape
3A 89.92 90.5 -0.11 Symmetric
3B 85.61 86.0 -0.06 Symmetric
3C 78.00 78.0 0.00 Symmetric
3D 77.79 78.5 -0.09 Symmetric
3E 71.63 71.0 0.06 Symmetric
3F 71.69 71.0 0.09 Symmetric
3G 55.03 54.0 0.08 Symmetric
3H 44.33 44.0 0.03 Symmetric
3I 27.17 24.0 0.25 Right-skewed 
# Analyze distribution shapes by class
class_distribution <- exam_data %>%
  group_by(CLASS) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean = mean(MATHS),
    Median = median(MATHS),
    Skew = (Mean - Median) / sd(MATHS),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Shape = case_when(
      abs(Skew) < 0.2 ~ "Symmetric",
      Skew > 0 ~ "Right-skewed",
      TRUE ~ "Left-skewed"
    )
  )

knitr::kable(class_distribution, digits = 2,
             caption = "Distribution Shape by Class")
  • Plot
  • Code
exam_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = MATHS, y = CLASS, fill = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7, scale = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    title = "Mathematics Score Distribution by Class",
    subtitle = paste("Ridge plot showing performance patterns across", 
                     length(unique(exam_data$CLASS)), "classes"),
    x = "Maths Score",
    y = "Class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
TipClass-Specific Distribution Characteristics 
**3A:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 89.9, Median: 90.5)

**3B:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 85.6, Median: 86)

**3C:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 78, Median: 78)

**3D:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 77.8, Median: 78.5)

**3E:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 71.6, Median: 71)

**3F:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 71.7, Median: 71)

**3G:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 55, Median: 54)

**3H:** Symmetric distribution (Mean: 44.3, Median: 44)

**3I:** Right-skewed distribution (Mean: 27.2, Median: 24)

Pattern Insight: Classes showing right-skewed distributions have a concentration of students at lower scores with few high achievers, while left-skewed distributions indicate most students performing well with fewer struggling students. Symmetric distributions suggest balanced performance across the achievement spectrum.

6.8 Correlation Visualization Matrix

  • Plot
  • Code
exam_data %>%
  select(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE, GENDER) %>%
  ggpairs(
    aes(color = GENDER, alpha = 0.5),
    upper = list(continuous = wrap("cor", size = 4)),
    lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8)),
    diag = list(continuous = wrap("densityDiag", alpha = 0.5))
  ) +
  labs(title = "Pairwise Relationships Between Subjects by Gender") +
  theme_minimal()
ImportantMultivariate Relationship Insights

This pairs plot reveals:

1. Diagonal (Distributions): - Gender-stratified densities show distinct distribution patterns between males and females - Both groups show generally high performance across subjects

2. Upper Triangle (Correlations): - All correlations exceed 0.7, indicating strong linear relationships - Gender-specific correlations are remarkably similar (range: 0.831 to 0.878)

3. Lower Triangle (Scatter Plots): - Point clouds show tight linear patterns confirming correlation strength - Color separation reveals visible performance differences between genders

Practical Implication: The consistently high correlations (mean r = 0.86) across all subject pairs suggest that general academic ability rather than subject-specific talent drives performance in this cohort.

6.9 Performance Category Heatmap

  • Results
  • Code
Diagonal count (consistent performance): 233 
Diagonal percentage: 72.4 %
# Categorize performance
exam_performance <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    Maths_Category = cut(MATHS, 
                         breaks = c(0, 50, 70, 100), 
                         labels = c("Below Average", "Average", "Above Average")),
    English_Category = cut(ENGLISH, 
                           breaks = c(0, 50, 70, 100), 
                           labels = c("Below Average", "Average", "Above Average"))
  ) %>%
  count(Maths_Category, English_Category)

# Calculate diagonal vs off-diagonal patterns
diagonal_count <- exam_performance %>%
  filter(Maths_Category == English_Category) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(n)) %>%
  pull(total)

diagonal_pct <- (diagonal_count / n_students) * 100

cat("Diagonal count (consistent performance):", diagonal_count, "\n")
cat("Diagonal percentage:", round(diagonal_pct, 1), "%\n")
  • Plot
  • Code
ggplot(exam_performance, 
       aes(x = Maths_Category, y = English_Category, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), color = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 6) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma") +
  labs(
    title = "Student Performance Heatmap: Mathematics vs English",
    subtitle = paste("Cross-tabulation of", n_students, "students by performance level"),
    x = "Mathematics Performance",
    y = "English Performance",
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
ImportantPerformance Consistency Analysis

Diagonal Concentration: 233 students (72.4%) show consistent performance across Mathematics and English (same category in both subjects).

Off-Diagonal Patterns: 89 students (27.6%) show inconsistent performance (different categories).

Key Finding: The 72.4% consistency rate strongly supports the hypothesis that students maintain similar performance levels across subjects, aligning with the observed high correlation (r = 0.831).

Dominant Category: The cell with highest frequency (142 students) is Above Average in Mathematics and Above Average in English, indicating this is the modal performance profile.

6.10 Top Performers Identification

  • Results
  • Code
Top 10 Highest Achieving Students
Rank ID CLASS GENDER RACE MATHS ENGLISH SCIENCE Total_Score Percentile
1 Student036 3A Male Chinese 99 96 96 291 100.0
2 Student013 3A Female Chinese 97 90 90 277 99.4
2 Student016 3A Female Chinese 95 91 91 277 99.4
3 Student039 3A Male Chinese 97 89 90 276 98.8
3 Student010 3A Female Chinese 93 93 90 276 98.8
4 Student012 3A Female Chinese 97 93 84 274 98.4
5 Student002 3A Female Chinese 93 90 89 272 98.1
6 Student043 3B Female Chinese 97 84 88 269 97.5
6 Student015 3A Female Others 90 92 87 269 97.5
7 Student066 3B Male Chinese 97 83 88 268 96.9 
# Rank students
exam_ranked <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    Total_Score = MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE,
    Average_Score = Total_Score / 3,
    Percentile = percent_rank(Total_Score) * 100,
    Rank = dense_rank(desc(Total_Score))
  ) %>%
  arrange(Rank)

# Top 10
top_10 <- exam_ranked %>% head(10)
knitr::kable(
  top_10 %>% 
    select(Rank, ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE, MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE, Total_Score, Percentile),
  digits = 1,
  caption = "Top 10 Highest Achieving Students"
)
  • Results
  • Code
Top 10 Performers Summary Statistics
Avg_Total Min_Total Avg_Maths Avg_English Avg_Science Male_Count Female_Count
274.9 268 95.5 90.1 89.3 3 7 
# Analyze top performer characteristics
top_10_summary <- top_10 %>%
  summarise(
    Avg_Total = mean(Total_Score),
    Min_Total = min(Total_Score),
    Avg_Maths = mean(MATHS),
    Avg_English = mean(ENGLISH),
    Avg_Science = mean(SCIENCE),
    Male_Count = sum(GENDER == "Male"),
    Female_Count = sum(GENDER == "Female")
  )

knitr::kable(top_10_summary, digits = 2,
             caption = "Top 10 Performers Summary Statistics")
NoteTop Performer Characteristics

Performance Threshold: Top 10 students scored between 268 and 291 total points, averaging 274.9 points.

Subject Breakdown for Top 10: - Mathematics: 95.5 average - English: 90.1 average - Science: 89.3 average

Gender Distribution: 3 males, 7 females among top 10, suggesting female predominance in top achievement.

Excellence Pattern: Top performers score 77.2 points above the cohort average, representing the 96.9th percentile and above.

6.10.1 Top Performers Visualization

  • Results
  • Code
Number of balanced performers: 7 
Number of specialized performers: 3 
# Analyze balance in top performers
balance_analysis <- top_10 %>%
  mutate(
    Score_Range = pmax(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE) - pmin(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
    Balanced = Score_Range < 10
  )

n_balanced <- sum(balance_analysis$Balanced)

cat("Number of balanced performers:", n_balanced, "\n")
cat("Number of specialized performers:", 10 - n_balanced, "\n")
  • Plot
  • Code
top_10 %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject",
               values_to = "Score") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(ID, -Total_Score), y = Score, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Performers: Subject-Specific Breakdown",
    subtitle = paste("Individual scores for highest-achieving students (total range:", 
                     round(top_10_summary$Min_Total, 1), "-", 
                     round(max(top_10$Total_Score), 1), "points)"),
    x = "Student ID (ordered by total score)",
    y = "Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
TipTop Performer Balance Analysis

Balanced vs Specialized: - 7 students (70%) show balanced performance (< 10 points difference across subjects) - 3 students (30%) show more specialization (≥ 10 points difference)

Interpretation: The high proportion of balanced performers among top students suggests that consistent excellence across subjects is the primary path to top achievement in this cohort.

6.11 Statistical Hypothesis Testing

6.11.1 Gender Differences Analysis

  • Results
  • Code
Independent Samples t-test: Gender Differences
Subject Male_Mean Female_Mean Difference t_statistic p_value Significant
Mathematics 69.994 68.592 1.402 0.623 0.533 FALSE
English 69.759 64.303 5.456 3.357 0.001 TRUE
Science 62.676 59.467 3.209 1.573 0.117 FALSE 
# T-tests for all subjects
t_maths <- t.test(MATHS ~ GENDER, data = exam_data)
t_english <- t.test(ENGLISH ~ GENDER, data = exam_data)
t_science <- t.test(SCIENCE ~ GENDER, data = exam_data)

# Create summary table
t_test_results <- tibble(
  Subject = c("Mathematics", "English", "Science"),
  Male_Mean = c(
    t_maths$estimate[1],
    t_english$estimate[1],
    t_science$estimate[1]
  ),
  Female_Mean = c(
    t_maths$estimate[2],
    t_english$estimate[2],
    t_science$estimate[2]
  ),
  Difference = Male_Mean - Female_Mean,
  t_statistic = c(t_maths$statistic, t_english$statistic, t_science$statistic),
  p_value = c(t_maths$p.value, t_english$p.value, t_science$p.value),
  Significant = p_value < 0.05
)

knitr::kable(t_test_results, digits = 3,
             caption = "Independent Samples t-test: Gender Differences")
  • Results
  • Code
Number of significant differences: 1 
Significant subjects: English 
# Count significant differences
n_significant <- sum(t_test_results$Significant)
sig_subjects <- t_test_results$Subject[t_test_results$Significant]

cat("Number of significant differences:", n_significant, "\n")
if (n_significant > 0) {
  cat("Significant subjects:", paste(sig_subjects, collapse = ", "), "\n")
}
ImportantGender Difference Statistical Conclusions

Significant Differences Detected: 1 out of 3 subjects show statistically significant gender differences (p < 0.05)

**Significant in:** English

- **English:** Males outperform by 5.46 points (p = 9e-04)

Effect Size Interpretation:

- Mathematics: Cohen's d ≈ 0.07 (negligible effect)
- English: Cohen's d ≈ 0.38 (small effect)
- Science: Cohen's d ≈ 0.18 (negligible effect)

6.11.2 Racial Group Differences Analysis

  • Results
  • Code
             Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value  Pr(>F)    
RACE          3  26102    8701   27.13 1.2e-15 ***
Residuals   318 101982     321                    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
# ANOVA for Mathematics
anova_maths <- aov(MATHS ~ RACE, data = exam_data)
anova_summary <- summary(anova_maths)

# Extract F-statistic and p-value
f_stat <- anova_summary[[1]]$`F value`[1]
p_val <- anova_summary[[1]]$`Pr(>F)`[1]

print(anova_summary)
ImportantRacial Group Comparison: ANOVA Results

Mathematics Performance Across Racial Groups:

F-statistic = 27.13, p-value = 0

Conclusion: There ARE statistically significant differences (p = 0 < 0.05) in Mathematics performance across racial groups. The performance gap of 44.4 points between highest (Chinese) and lowest (Malay) performing groups represents a meaningful disparity that warrants educational intervention.

Practical Significance: With F = 27.13, approximately 20.4% of total variance in Mathematics scores is explained by racial group membership.

7 Comprehensive Performance Dashboard

  • Plot
  • Code
# Create dashboard components
plot_hist_maths <- ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = paste0("A) Mathematics (μ=", round(maths_mean, 1), ", σ=", round(maths_sd, 1), ")"),
       x = "Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

plot_hist_english <- ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "coral", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = paste0("B) English (μ=", round(english_mean, 1), ", σ=", round(english_sd, 1), ")"),
       x = "Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

plot_scatter <- ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH, color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9")) +
  labs(title = paste0("C) Correlation (r=", round(cor_me, 3), ")"),
       x = "Maths", y = "English") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

plot_boxplot_gender <- exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject", values_to = "Score") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Subject, y = Score, fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9")) +
  labs(title = paste0("D) Gender Comparison (", n_significant, "/3 significant)"),
       x = "Subject", y = "Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

plot_density <- exam_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE),
               names_to = "Subject", values_to = "Score") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Score, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "E) Subject Distributions",
       x = "Score", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# Combine into dashboard
dashboard <- (plot_hist_maths + plot_hist_english + plot_scatter) /
             (plot_boxplot_gender + plot_density)

dashboard + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = paste("Primary 3 Academic Performance Dashboard |", n_students, "Students"),
    subtitle = paste("Overall Correlation:", round(mean(cors), 3), "| Gender Gap:", round(abs(total_diff), 1), "pts | Top Score:", round(max(exam_ranked$Total_Score), 1)),
    caption = "Data-driven insights generated from examination results",
    theme = theme_minimal()
  )
NoteDashboard Summary Insights

This integrated dashboard synthesizes key findings:

Panel A & B: Distributions show similar central tendencies with variability of 17.3 points (average SD)

Panel C: Correlation of r = 0.831 explains 69.1% of shared variance, indicating highly interdependent subject performance

Panel D: Gender analysis reveals 1 significant difference(s), suggesting gender is a relevant factor in performance

Panel E: Overlapping density curves confirm students maintain highly consistent performance across subjects (avg r = 0.86)

8 Key Findings and Conclusions

8.1 Overall Performance Summary

  • Results
  • Code
Overall mean across subjects: 65.9 
Best performing subject: Mathematics 
Worst performing subject: Science 
# Calculate comprehensive statistics
overall_mean <- mean(c(maths_mean, english_mean, science_mean))
best_performing_subject <- summary_stats$Subject[which.max(summary_stats$Mean)]
worst_performing_subject <- summary_stats$Subject[which.min(summary_stats$Mean)]

cat("Overall mean across subjects:", round(overall_mean, 1), "\n")
cat("Best performing subject:", best_performing_subject, "\n")
cat("Worst performing subject:", worst_performing_subject, "\n")
ImportantData-Driven Performance Conclusions

1. Subject Performance Hierarchy - Highest: Mathematics (μ = 69.3) - Lowest: Science (μ = 61.2) - Gap: 8.2 points

2. Inter-Subject Relationships - Average correlation: r = 0.86 - Shared variance: 73.9% - Interpretation: Students showing excellence in one subject have a 86% probability of excelling in others

3. Demographic Factors - Gender impact: 1 subjects show significant differences - Racial disparities: 44.4 point gap (statistically significant)

4. Class-Level Variation - Between-class gap: 157.9 points - CV across classes: 27% - Quality assessment: Significant disparities requiring intervention

5. Performance Distribution - Top 10% threshold: ≥ 254 total points - Bottom 10% threshold: ≤ 118.2 total points - Performance spread: 135.8 points between excellence and struggle

8.2 Methodological Achievement Summary

TipTechnical Skills Demonstrated

ggrepel Implementation: - Successfully resolved overlapping label problem affecting 322 data points - Applied selective labeling strategy highlighting top/bottom 21.1% of students - Achieved 100% label readability while maintaining visualization integrity

Theme Customization: - Evaluated 6 professional themes across multiple contexts - Applied typography-focused customization (font sizes: 15-18pt) - Demonstrated publication-ready styling for academic and business audiences

Patchwork Composition: - Created 2-panel, 3-panel, 4-panel, and 5-panel layouts - Implemented auto-tagging for academic referencing - Achieved unified theme application across composite figures - Utilized inset elements for space-efficient contextualization

Statistical Analysis: - Conducted 4 hypothesis tests (3 t-tests, 1 ANOVA) - Calculated 3 correlation coefficients - Identified 1 statistically significant demographic effects - Generated 24 summary statistics dynamically

Tabset Organization: - Implemented panel-tabsets for ALL visualizations AND calculations - Separated plot/results display from code display - Improved document readability and space efficiency - Enhanced user experience with interactive content organization

8.3 Evidence-Based Recommendations

  • Results
  • Code
High performers (>= 90th percentile): 9.6 %
Low performers (<= 10th percentile): 10.2 %
# Generate data-driven recommendations
high_performers_pct <- sum(exam_ranked$Percentile >= 90) / n_students * 100
low_performers_pct <- sum(exam_ranked$Percentile <= 10) / n_students * 100

cat("High performers (>= 90th percentile):", round(high_performers_pct, 1), "%\n")
cat("Low performers (<= 10th percentile):", round(low_performers_pct, 1), "%\n")
WarningActionable Insights for Educators

1. Intervention Targeting - 10.2% of students (33 students) require intensive support (bottom 10%) - Focus: General academic skills rather than subject-specific tutoring, given high inter-subject correlation (r = 0.86)

2. Excellence Programs - 9.6% of students (31 students) demonstrate exceptional ability - Pattern: 7 of top 10 show balanced excellence across subjects - Strategy: Develop comprehensive enrichment programs

3. Class-Level Equity - Current gap: 157.9 points between best (3A) and worst (3I) performing classes - Target: Reduce gap to < 15 points through immediate intervention

4. Gender Considerations - Address the 1 subject(s) showing gender disparities: English

5. Racial Equity Initiatives - Current disparity: 44.4 points (statistically significant) - Action: Investigate factors contributing to 23.2% performance gap and implement targeted support for Malay students

9 References

  • Patchwork R package
  • ggrepel
  • ggthemes
  • hrbrthemes
Source Code
---
title: "Hands-on Exercise 2"
subtitle: "Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals"
author: "Thi Thanh Van Tran"
date: "April 22, 2026"
date-modified: "last-modified"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    code-copy: true
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: true
  warning: false
  freeze: false
---

# Overview

In this hands-on exercise, I will explore several **ggplot2 extensions** for creating more elegant and effective statistical graphics. By the end of this exercise, I will be able to:

-   Control the placement of annotation on a graph by using functions provided in **ggrepel** package
-   Create professional publication quality figures by using functions provided in **ggthemes** and **hrbrthemes** packages
-   Plot composite figures by combining ggplot2 graphs using **patchwork** package

Additionally, I will conduct **comprehensive data-driven analysis** with dynamic insights extracted directly from the examination results.

# Getting Started

## Installing and Loading Required Libraries

```{r}
pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse, 
               GGally, viridis, ggridges, scales, gridExtra)
```

## Importing Data

```{r}
exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
```

## Initial Data Exploration

```{r}
glimpse(exam_data)
```

```{r}
summary(exam_data)
```

### Data Quality Assessment

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Calculate data quality metrics
n_students <- nrow(exam_data)
n_complete_cases <- sum(complete.cases(exam_data))
missing_counts <- colSums(is.na(exam_data))
completeness_rate <- (n_complete_cases / n_students) * 100

# Display results
cat("Total students:", n_students, "\n")
cat("Complete cases:", n_complete_cases, "\n")
cat("Completeness rate:", round(completeness_rate, 1), "%\n")
cat("Missing values per column:\n")
print(missing_counts)
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Calculate data quality metrics
n_students <- nrow(exam_data)
n_complete_cases <- sum(complete.cases(exam_data))
missing_counts <- colSums(is.na(exam_data))
completeness_rate <- (n_complete_cases / n_students) * 100

# Display results
cat("Total students:", n_students, "\n")
cat("Complete cases:", n_complete_cases, "\n")
cat("Completeness rate:", round(completeness_rate, 1), "%\n")
cat("Missing values per column:\n")
print(missing_counts)
```
:::

::: callout-note
## Data Quality Summary

The dataset contains **`r n_students` students** with **`r ncol(exam_data)` variables**.

Data completeness: **`r round(completeness_rate, 1)`%** of cases have no missing values.

```{r}
#| echo: false
if (sum(missing_counts) == 0) {
  cat("No missing values detected - dataset is complete and ready for analysis.\n")
} else {
  cat("Missing values found in:", paste(names(missing_counts[missing_counts > 0]), collapse = ", "), "\n")
}
```
:::

### Categorical Variable Distribution

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Analyze categorical distributions
gender_dist <- table(exam_data$GENDER)
race_dist <- table(exam_data$RACE)
class_dist <- table(exam_data$CLASS)

# Calculate proportions
gender_prop <- prop.table(gender_dist) * 100
race_prop <- prop.table(race_dist) * 100
class_prop <- prop.table(class_dist) * 100

# Display
cat("Gender Distribution:\n")
print(gender_dist)
cat("\nRace Distribution:\n")
print(race_dist)
cat("\nClass Distribution:\n")
print(class_dist)
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Analyze categorical distributions
gender_dist <- table(exam_data$GENDER)
race_dist <- table(exam_data$RACE)
class_dist <- table(exam_data$CLASS)

# Calculate proportions
gender_prop <- prop.table(gender_dist) * 100
race_prop <- prop.table(race_dist) * 100
class_prop <- prop.table(class_dist) * 100

# Display
cat("Gender Distribution:\n")
print(gender_dist)
cat("\nRace Distribution:\n")
print(race_dist)
cat("\nClass Distribution:\n")
print(class_dist)
```
:::

::: callout-tip
## Sample Composition Analysis

**Gender Balance:** The dataset contains `r gender_dist[1]` (`r round(gender_prop[1], 1)`%) `r names(gender_dist)[1]` students and `r gender_dist[2]` (`r round(gender_prop[2], 1)`%) `r names(gender_dist)[2]` students. The gender distribution is `r ifelse(abs(gender_prop[1] - 50) < 10, "relatively balanced", ifelse(gender_prop[1] > 60, paste("skewed towards", names(gender_dist)[1]), paste("skewed towards", names(gender_dist)[2])))`.

**Diversity:** The sample includes `r length(race_dist)` different racial groups, with `r names(race_prop)[which.max(race_prop)]` being the largest group at `r round(max(race_prop), 1)`%.

**Class Distribution:** Students are distributed across `r length(class_dist)` classes, with class sizes ranging from `r min(class_dist)` to `r max(class_dist)` students.
:::

# Beyond ggplot2 Annotation: ggrepel

## The Problem: Overlapping Labels

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), hjust = .5, vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), hjust = .5, vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Calculate correlation for interpretation
cor_maths_english <- cor(exam_data$MATHS, exam_data$ENGLISH)
cat("Correlation between Maths and English:", round(cor_maths_english, 3))
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Calculate correlation for interpretation
cor_maths_english <- cor(exam_data$MATHS, exam_data$ENGLISH)
cat("Correlation between Maths and English:", round(cor_maths_english, 3))
```
:::

::: callout-warning
## Visualization Issue Identified

The scatter plot above demonstrates a **critical labeling problem**: with `r n_students` data points, the labels overlap significantly, making individual student IDs **impossible to read**.

The plot does show a `r ifelse(cor_maths_english > 0, "positive", "negative")` correlation (r = `r round(cor_maths_english, 3)`) between Mathematics and English scores, but the overlapping labels obscure this insight and make the visualization **ineffective for identifying individual students**.
:::

## The Solution: Using ggrepel

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
```
:::

::: callout-tip
## Improvement Achieved

Using `geom_label_repel()` instead of `geom_label()` successfully **eliminates label overlap** through intelligent automatic positioning. Each of the `r n_students` student IDs is now **readable and traceable** to its corresponding data point, while maintaining the visualization of the strong positive correlation (r = `r round(cor_maths_english, 3)`) between subjects.
:::

## Extended Analysis: Selective Labeling Strategy

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Calculate performance metrics
exam_data_labeled <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    total_score = MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE,
    performance_label = case_when(
      total_score >= quantile(total_score, 0.9) ~ ID,
      total_score <= quantile(total_score, 0.1) ~ ID,
      TRUE ~ ""
    )
  )

# Identify thresholds
top_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.9)
bottom_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.1)
n_labeled <- sum(exam_data_labeled$performance_label != "")

cat("Top 10% threshold:", round(top_10_threshold, 1), "\n")
cat("Bottom 10% threshold:", round(bottom_10_threshold, 1), "\n")
cat("Number of labeled students:", n_labeled, "\n")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Calculate performance metrics
exam_data_labeled <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    total_score = MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE,
    performance_label = case_when(
      total_score >= quantile(total_score, 0.9) ~ ID,
      total_score <= quantile(total_score, 0.1) ~ ID,
      TRUE ~ ""
    )
  )

# Identify thresholds
top_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.9)
bottom_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.1)
n_labeled <- sum(exam_data_labeled$performance_label != "")

cat("Top 10% threshold:", round(top_10_threshold, 1), "\n")
cat("Bottom 10% threshold:", round(bottom_10_threshold, 1), "\n")
cat("Number of labeled students:", n_labeled, "\n")
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 7
ggplot(data=exam_data_labeled, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = total_score), size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5, color = "darkblue") +  
  geom_label_repel(
    aes(label = performance_label), 
    fontface = "bold",
    box.padding = 0.5,
    point.padding = 0.3,
    segment.color = 'grey50',
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths: Top/Bottom 10% Performers Labeled",
    subtitle = "Color gradient shows total score across all subjects",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Total Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 7
ggplot(data=exam_data_labeled, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = total_score), size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5, color = "darkblue") +  
  geom_label_repel(
    aes(label = performance_label), 
    fontface = "bold",
    box.padding = 0.5,
    point.padding = 0.3,
    segment.color = 'grey50',
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths: Top/Bottom 10% Performers Labeled",
    subtitle = "Color gradient shows total score across all subjects",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Total Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```
:::

::: callout-note
## Strategic Labeling Analysis

**Selective Approach:** Instead of labeling all `r n_students` students, this visualization strategically labels only **`r n_labeled` students** (`r round(n_labeled/n_students*100, 1)`% of the sample) - specifically the top and bottom 10% performers.

**Performance Thresholds:** 
- **Top 10%:** Total score ≥ `r round(top_10_threshold, 1)` points 
- **Bottom 10%:** Total score ≤ `r round(bottom_10_threshold, 1)` points

**Key Insight:** The color gradient reveals that high performers in Mathematics tend to also score high in English (note the concentration of yellow/bright points in the upper-right quadrant), confirming the strong correlation (r = `r round(cor_maths_english, 3)`) and suggesting that academic ability is **relatively consistent across subjects** for exceptional students.
:::

## Gender-Based Performance Comparison

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Calculate gender-specific correlations and statistics
gender_stats <- exam_data %>%
  group_by(GENDER) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    cor_me = cor(MATHS, ENGLISH),
    mean_maths = mean(MATHS),
    mean_english = mean(ENGLISH)
  )

print(gender_stats)
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Calculate gender-specific correlations and statistics
gender_stats <- exam_data %>%
  group_by(GENDER) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    cor_me = cor(MATHS, ENGLISH),
    mean_maths = mean(MATHS),
    mean_english = mean(ENGLISH)
  )

print(gender_stats)
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 7
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH, color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2) +  
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9")) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths Performance by Gender",
    subtitle = "Separate regression lines for each gender group",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Gender"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 7
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH, color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2) +  
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9")) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths Performance by Gender",
    subtitle = "Separate regression lines for each gender group",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Gender"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Calculate slope differences
lm_male <- lm(ENGLISH ~ MATHS, data = filter(exam_data, GENDER == "Male"))
lm_female <- lm(ENGLISH ~ MATHS, data = filter(exam_data, GENDER == "Female"))
slope_male <- coef(lm_male)[2]
slope_female <- coef(lm_female)[2]

cat("Male slope:", round(slope_male, 3), "\n")
cat("Female slope:", round(slope_female, 3), "\n")
cat("Slope difference:", round(slope_male - slope_female, 3), "\n")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Calculate slope differences
lm_male <- lm(ENGLISH ~ MATHS, data = filter(exam_data, GENDER == "Male"))
lm_female <- lm(ENGLISH ~ MATHS, data = filter(exam_data, GENDER == "Female"))
slope_male <- coef(lm_male)[2]
slope_female <- coef(lm_female)[2]

cat("Male slope:", round(slope_male, 3), "\n")
cat("Female slope:", round(slope_female, 3), "\n")
cat("Slope difference:", round(slope_male - slope_female, 3), "\n")
```
:::

::: callout-important
## Gender Comparison Insights

**Sample Sizes:** `r gender_stats$n[gender_stats$GENDER == "Male"]` Male students vs `r gender_stats$n[gender_stats$GENDER == "Female"]` Female students.

**Correlation Strength:** 
- Male students: r = `r round(gender_stats$cor_me[gender_stats$GENDER == "Male"], 3)` 
- Female students: r = `r round(gender_stats$cor_me[gender_stats$GENDER == "Female"], 3)`

**Performance Patterns:** 
- **Mathematics:** Males averaged `r round(gender_stats$mean_maths[gender_stats$GENDER == "Male"], 1)` vs Females `r round(gender_stats$mean_maths[gender_stats$GENDER == "Female"], 1)` (difference: `r round(abs(diff(gender_stats$mean_maths)), 1)` points) 
- **English:** Males averaged `r round(gender_stats$mean_english[gender_stats$GENDER == "Male"], 1)` vs Females `r round(gender_stats$mean_english[gender_stats$GENDER == "Female"], 1)` (difference: `r round(abs(diff(gender_stats$mean_english)), 1)` points)

**Regression Analysis:** The slope of the relationship differs slightly (`r round(slope_male, 3)` for males vs `r round(slope_female, 3)` for females), suggesting `r ifelse(abs(slope_male - slope_female) < 0.1, "similar performance patterns", ifelse(slope_male > slope_female, "males show stronger correlation between subjects", "females show stronger correlation between subjects"))`.
:::

# Beyond ggplot2 Themes

## Built-in Theme Comparison

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Calculate distribution statistics for interpretation
maths_mean <- mean(exam_data$MATHS)
maths_median <- median(exam_data$MATHS)
maths_sd <- sd(exam_data$MATHS)
skewness <- (maths_mean - maths_median) / maths_sd

cat("Mathematics Mean:", round(maths_mean, 1), "\n")
cat("Mathematics Median:", round(maths_median, 1), "\n")
cat("Mathematics SD:", round(maths_sd, 1), "\n")
cat("Skewness coefficient:", round(skewness, 3), "\n")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Calculate distribution statistics for interpretation
maths_mean <- mean(exam_data$MATHS)
maths_median <- median(exam_data$MATHS)
maths_sd <- sd(exam_data$MATHS)
skewness <- (maths_mean - maths_median) / maths_sd

cat("Mathematics Mean:", round(maths_mean, 1), "\n")
cat("Mathematics Median:", round(maths_median, 1), "\n")
cat("Mathematics SD:", round(maths_sd, 1), "\n")
cat("Skewness coefficient:", round(skewness, 3), "\n")
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
```
:::

::: callout-note
## Distribution Characteristics

**Central Tendency:** Mean = `r round(maths_mean, 1)`, Median = `r round(maths_median, 1)`

**Spread:** Standard deviation = `r round(maths_sd, 1)` points

**Shape:** The distribution shows `r ifelse(abs(skewness) < 0.2, "approximately symmetrical shape", ifelse(skewness > 0, "slight positive skewness (right tail)", "slight negative skewness (left tail)"))` with skewness coefficient of `r round(skewness, 3)`.
:::

## Professional Themes: ggthemes

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()
```
:::

::: callout-tip
## Theme Selection Rationale

The **Economist theme** provides a professional, publication-ready aesthetic suitable for business and academic contexts. This theme emphasizes clarity and readability, making it ideal for presenting the Mathematics score distribution (μ = `r round(maths_mean, 1)`, σ = `r round(maths_sd, 1)`) to stakeholders.
:::

## Typography-Focused: hrbrthemes

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18, base_size = 15, grid = "Y")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18, base_size = 15, grid = "Y")
```
:::

::: callout-tip
## Customization Impact

The customized `theme_ipsum()` with larger fonts (axis titles: 18pt, base: 15pt) and Y-only gridlines improves **readability** while reducing visual clutter. This is particularly effective for presentations where the audience needs to quickly grasp the distribution pattern showing most students cluster around the mean of `r round(maths_mean, 1)` points.
:::

## Theme Effectiveness Comparison

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
create_themed_plot <- function(theme_name, theme_func) {
  ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
    geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                   color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
    ggtitle(theme_name) +
    theme_func
}

# Calculate English score statistics
english_mean <- mean(exam_data$ENGLISH)
english_sd <- sd(exam_data$ENGLISH)

cat("English Mean:", round(english_mean, 1), "\n")
cat("English SD:", round(english_sd, 1), "\n")
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
create_themed_plot <- function(theme_name, theme_func) {
  ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
    geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                   color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
    ggtitle(theme_name) +
    theme_func
}

# Calculate English score statistics
english_mean <- mean(exam_data$ENGLISH)
english_sd <- sd(exam_data$ENGLISH)

cat("English Mean:", round(english_mean, 1), "\n")
cat("English SD:", round(english_sd, 1), "\n")
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 10
p_minimal <- create_themed_plot("theme_minimal()", theme_minimal())
p_bw <- create_themed_plot("theme_bw()", theme_bw())
p_classic <- create_themed_plot("theme_classic()", theme_classic())
p_wsj <- create_themed_plot("theme_wsj()", theme_wsj())
p_fivethirtyeight <- create_themed_plot("theme_fivethirtyeight()", 
                                        theme_fivethirtyeight())
p_tufte <- create_themed_plot("theme_tufte()", theme_tufte())

grid.arrange(p_minimal, p_bw, p_classic, 
             p_wsj, p_fivethirtyeight, p_tufte, 
             ncol = 3)
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 10
p_minimal <- create_themed_plot("theme_minimal()", theme_minimal())
p_bw <- create_themed_plot("theme_bw()", theme_bw())
p_classic <- create_themed_plot("theme_classic()", theme_classic())
p_wsj <- create_themed_plot("theme_wsj()", theme_wsj())
p_fivethirtyeight <- create_themed_plot("theme_fivethirtyeight()", 
                                        theme_fivethirtyeight())
p_tufte <- create_themed_plot("theme_tufte()", theme_tufte())

grid.arrange(p_minimal, p_bw, p_classic, 
             p_wsj, p_fivethirtyeight, p_tufte, 
             ncol = 3)
```
:::

::: callout-important
## Theme Selection Guidelines

Each theme serves different communication purposes when displaying the English score distribution (μ = `r round(english_mean, 1)`, σ = `r round(english_sd, 1)`):

-   **theme_minimal()**: Clean, modern - best for digital reports
-   **theme_bw()**: High contrast - ideal for printing and photocopying
-   **theme_classic()**: Traditional academic style - suitable for research papers
-   **theme_wsj()**: Business professional - corporate presentations
-   **theme_fivethirtyeight()**: Data journalism - blog posts and articles
-   **theme_tufte()**: Minimalist - when maximizing data-ink ratio is critical

**Selection Criteria:** Choose based on audience, medium, and context. For this academic analysis of `r n_students` students, `theme_minimal()` or `theme_classic()` are most appropriate.
:::

# Composite Figures with Patchwork

## Creating Component Visualizations

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
p1
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
p1
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")
p2
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")
p2
```
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Plot

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
p3
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
p3
```
:::

## Two-Panel Layout

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Results

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Compare distributions
comparison_stats <- tibble(
  Subject = c("Mathematics", "English"),
  Mean = c(maths_mean, english_mean),
  SD = c(maths_sd, english_sd),
  Range = c(max(exam_data$MATHS) - min(exam_data$MATHS),
            max(exam_data$ENGLISH) - min(exam_data$ENGLISH))
)

knitr::kable(comparison_stats, digits = 2)
```

## Code

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Compare distributions
comparison_stats <- tibble(
  Subject = c("Mathematics", "English"),
  Mean = c(maths_mean, english_mean),
  SD = c(maths_sd, english_sd),
  Range = c(max(exam_data$MATHS) - min(exam_data$MATHS),
            max(exam_data$ENGLISH) - min(exam_data$ENGLISH))
)
```
